data.title: NovaGold: A Giant With Feet Of Clay
data.description:
Future Coverage

Over the next few months, I plan to cover the entire precious metals mining industry. I have already covered multiple explorers, miners, and royalty companies, but this is just the beginning. If you would like coverage on a particular name, please leave a comment below. Ultimately, I will try to cover them all, but I can fast-track those with more interest.

Rocks and junior exploration companies are what brought me to finance as a teenager, so I am simply going back to where everything started. This time around, I understand the unsaid rules of the game. If you don't know who the fool is around the poker table, it is likely you. To be honest, I have been a bagholder quite a few times, and I hope to share some of the lessons I have learned.

At heart, I am an explorer, a trailblazer. This is why I am drawn to mining, to commodities. Wild instead of synthetic. Authentic instead of fake. Truth over lies, and love over fear. The ultimate battle inside everyone's mind. The prize is the soul. The Holy Grail. The philosopher's stone.

NovaGold Summary

NovaGold Resources (NYSE:NG) is sitting on 20.3 million ounces of attributable proven and probable gold at an average grade of 2.09 g/t. While it may look impressive at first, the Donlin Gold Project carries a few fatal flaws. Given its size and remote location, massive infrastructure investments would be required. Even if capital costs were not an issue, I wonder if some parts of the project are even technically feasible, including the construction of ~500 kilometres-long pipelines across a mountain range. It is my personal opinion that Donlin is unlikely to ever be built. It is also important to note that Donlin is NovaGold's only material asset.

Kaplan, Paulson & Barrick

NovaGold owns a 60% interest in the Donlin Gold Project located in Alaska, with the remaining 40% owned by Paulson & Co., the family office of the legendary hedge fund manager John Paulson. He is well-known for shorting subprime mortgages through credit default swaps leading up to the 2008 Global Financial Crisis. He then got interested in gold, making another windfall during the 2010-2011 bull market in precious metals. This period marked the second time in history in which silver traded close to $50.00.

One thing is clear: silver's breakout above $50.00 will be historic, a market move for the ages. Clearing the $35.00 mark over the last few days was certainly good to see, but the real resistance remains $50.00. As discussed in a previous article about Pan American Silver (PAAS), it is quite impressive that silver is still trading well below its 1980 highs, despite a nearly 40-fold increase in the U.S. federal debt. I am also bullish on gold, but silver has a little something more. It is hard to describe, but it is something I can feel.

What if we return to the spring of 1980 when the Hunt brothers attempted to corner the silver market? In this era, the U.S. federal debt was still below $1 trillion, and in January 1980, silver prices reached their intraday COMEX all-time high of $50.35 per ounce. Since then, the U.S. federal debt has increased by almost 40x, but silver prices are still 35% below their highs.

In April 2025, NovaGold and Paulson announced the acquisition of Barrick's (B) 50% interest in the Donlin Gold Project for $1.0 billion in cash. Under the agreement, NovaGold acquired an additional 10% stake for $200 million, while the remaining 40% was purchased by Paulson & Co for $800 million. As illustrated below, Paulson was already NovaGold's second-largest shareholder, so on an effective basis, they are pretty much equal partners in the project.

The Electrum Group, NovaGold's largest shareholder with a 25.4% interest, is an entity controlled by Thomas Kaplan, another household name in natural resources investing. Among many other ventures, Mr. Kaplan founded Apex Silver Mines in 1993, the company behind the discovery of the San Cristobal Deposit in Bolivia. According to the 2025 World Silver Survey, the San Cristobal Mine is the 5th largest primary silver mine in the world, with a production of 16.8 million ounces.

Barrick Selling, Why?

Barrick Mining, the world's second-largest gold producer, just sold its 50% interest in Donlin, a massive undeveloped gold deposit with proven and probable reserves estimated at ~33.8 million ounces of gold with an average grade of 2.09 g/t. Like any other mining company, Barrick is running on a depletion treadmill, and Donlin would have been one of the few projects with enough scale to move the needle on Barrick's overall gold production volume.

Donlin's divestiture came just a few weeks after 'Barrick Gold' rebranded itself into 'Barrick Mining', a change officializing a strategic shift toward copper. I remember the rumours of a potential merger between Barrick and First Quantum (FM:CA) in January 2024, a few months after Cobre Panama was forced to stop operations due to ongoing tensions with local communities.

If we go further back to early 2020, Barrick was also eying a possible acquisition of Freeport's (FCX) Grasberg copper and gold mine in Indonesia. I researched Freeport back in 2019 when I was still an investment analyst in Toronto. This is when I realized that Grasberg is one of the most impressive assets in the entire mining industry. Being the world's second-largest gold mine is already an achievement, but Grasberg, comes on top of being the world's second-largest copper mine. Open-pit operations at Grasberg began in 1990, and after ~35 years of continuous production, production volumes are higher than ever. In July 2023, Grasberg broke a world record after mining 151,582 tons of ore in a single day. Impressive! I discussed the importance of mine-life in more detail in an article about Altius Minerals (ALS:CA) last year, and Grasberg is another great example.

Any analyst building an NPV model on the Rocanville mine in 1970 when it entered production would have likely missed the target as the mine is not only still producing almost 55 years later, but the mine is actually bigger than ever after an important round of expansion in 2013. In my view, this is where the big money is made.

Let's not forget that Barrick is also busy with the construction of the Reko Diq Project in Pakistan. First production is expected before the end of 2028 after initial investments of ~$5.5 billion. So, on top of switching its focus to copper, Barrick is already funding the construction of a megaproject in Pakistan. In this context, would it have made sense for Barrick to launch a gold megaproject in the middle of nowhere, in Alaska? I don't think so. Instead, Barrick will use the $1.0 billion from Donlin to strengthen its balance sheet and to fund its copper ambitions. However, it still raises a question: If Donlin was such a world-class asset, why would Barrick sell?

Paulson & Kaplan Buying, Why?

In numerous interviews, Thomas Kaplan was clear that the Donlin Project was his way of gaining long-term optionality to higher gold prices. In the past, John Paulson employed a similar strategy with International Tower Hill Mines (THM) and the Livengood Project, also located in Alaska. In THM's case, we are talking about 9.0 million ounces of proven and probable gold at an average grade of 0.65 g/t, which is much lower than Donlin's average grade above 2.0 g/t. Livengood is also located in a remote area, where significant infrastructure investments would be required before any form of mining can take place.

As of May 2025, Paulson owned approximately 33.8% of International Tower Hill Mines. On the shareholder list, we also find that Kaplan's Electrum group is THM's second-largest shareholder, holding a 13.3% stake. In other words, we find the same two partners, Paulson and Kaplan, controlling another large deposit in the middle of nowhere in Alaska. Both deposits have been known for a long time, but remain undeveloped. Why? What is the strategy there?

When Barrick announced the sale of its 50% interest in Donlin, there was one element in particular that caught my attention. In the initial press release of April 2025, it was mentioned that NovaGold obtained funding commitments of up to $170 million at $3.00 per share from Paulson, The Electrum Group, and Kopernik Global Investors. That was intended to serve as a backstop to finance the purchase of a 10% additional stake in Donlin for $200 million, while NovaGold was looking for better financing alternatives. In the end, NovaGold raised net proceeds of $234.1 million via the issuance of shares at $3.75.

So did Paulson, The Electrum Group and Kopernik Global Investors provide this backstop for free, out of charity? Of course not. In exchange for the backstop, those three shareholders received five-year warrants to purchase a total of 25.5 million common shares at $3.00. In the regulatory filings, they are referred to as 'Fee Warrants'. In other words, NovaGold's shareholders first got diluted at $3.00 per share through warrants associated with the financial backstop, and then at $3.75 with the public offering and private placement. Those five-year warrants are not publicly listed and were not offered to minority shareholders. For me, it is an indication of 'Agency Risk', meaning that the entities controlling Donlin may not necessarily have the same financial incentives as those of minority shareholders.

As a reminder, NovaGold does not generate any revenues and will require a significant amount of capital over the next few years just to keep Donlin in good standing. With cheap warrants issued as compensation for financial backing, NovaGold's largest shareholders can gradually increase their ownership interest over time, while diluting the minority shareholders who do not have access to the same warrants. So yeah, when I think about NovaGold's future capital needs, I can't help but think about those cheap 'Fee Warrants'.

Donlin Project Economics

According to Donlin's most recent Technical Report, initial capital costs were estimated at $7.4 billion when using 2020 price estimates. To put this number in perspective, initial capital costs were estimated at $6.7 billion in a 2011 Feasibility Study. Considering the historic inflation wave we have all experienced since 2011, how much would it cost to build Donlin today? On this topic, I can think of Eldorado Gold (EGO) revising Skouries' construction costs twice over the last few quarters. What about B2Gold (BTG) and its Goose Project in Nunavut? If intermediate producers are already dealing with cost overruns, what would happen to a megaproject like Donlin? Instead of talking in hundreds of millions, Donlin's cost overruns could be in billions. How many mining companies have the balance sheet capacity to deal with this risk?

In terms of production volume, Donlin could become one of the world's largest gold mines, with an average annual production exceeding 1.1 million ounces over 27 years. What is also impressive is that the entire 39.0 million ounces in measured and indicated resources were delineated with less than 350,000 meters of drilling.

As discussed in a recent article, Amex Exploration (AMX:CA) reported a mere 1.6 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 6.14 g/t after drilling ~600,000 meters on the Perron Property. Few parallels can be made between Donlin and Perron, as they are at both extremes of the industry's spectrum. However, when juxtaposed, they highlight the importance of drilling efficiency. With a single meter of drilling, how much measured and indicated resource can you add to a project? In NovaGold's case, every meter of drilling added more than 110 ounces of gold, compared to only ~2.7 ounces for Amex. The less efficient you are with the drill, the more capital you will need to delineate an economic deposit, if ever.

Being efficient with the drill bit is great, but in NovaGold's case, it also raises some questions. Since its foundation, NovaGold has raised almost $2.0 billion in capital. With less than 350,000 meters of drilling completed at Donlin, where did it all go?

Do I think that Paulson and Kaplan want to bring Donlin into production? Even if they wanted to do so and had all the required capital, I doubt it would even be possible from a technical and engineering perspective. In my view, Donlin must be viewed as a decaying financial instrument rather than a future cash-generating asset. As discussed in more detail below, this financial instrument has its own behaviour and is not necessarily correlated to gold prices.

Before talking about cash flows and net present values, massive infrastructure investments would have to take place, including marine and river ports, a permanent camp, an airstrip, and a ~500-kilometre-long natural gas pipeline, among others. Given all the geopolitical and monetary uncertainties ahead, I would much rather invest in smaller projects with fast paybacks than a megaproject like Donlin, which requires an investment horizon measured in decades.

In the last week, I read the book 'Fiat Money Inflation In France' written by Andrew Dickson White in 1876. As inflation progressed in France, investment horizons became shorter and shorter, and speculative manias became the norm. In today's economic environment, can we witness similar patterns? Yes, I think so. The implications on Donlin's attractiveness are direct, given that a multi-decade investment horizon becomes a prerequisite with such a large-scale project.

None felt any confidence in the future in any respect; few dared to make a business investment for any length of time and it was accounted a folly to curtail the pleasures of the moment, to accumulate or save for so uncertain a future.

Another element I would like to highlight about Donlin is its metallurgy. Yes, an average grade of ~2.0 g/t is impressive for such a large open-pit project. However, we are dealing with refractory ore here, so gold recovery is not as straightforward as it may seem. According to the most recent technical reports, gold recovery was estimated at 89.8% after autoclave and carbon-in-leach treatment. I am not saying that metallurgy is a dealbreaker, but it is certainly an element to keep in mind when looking at the deposit's seemingly impressive average grade. What Mother Nature gave in terms of ounces was, in part, taken back by a less-than-ideal metallurgy. The consulting firm McKinsey wrote an interesting report on the topic of refractory ore in March 2021. In general terms, refractory deposits are higher grade and located in lower-risk jurisdictions, but that comes with a more difficult metallurgy. That's the trade-off.

As most refractory reserves and resources lie in jurisdictions with low mining-regulatory and political risk, risk-averse companies may instead face geological risks associated with refractory-type deposits.

Conclusion

Using today's prices, Donlin's construction costs could easily surpass $10 billion, and its hefty price tag comes with serious engineering challenges, including a ~500-kilometre pipeline through a mountain range in one of the most inhospitable places on Earth. The pipeline construction costs of $1.1 billion included in the most recent Technical Report are based on a 2011 estimate, then adjusted for inflation.

In my view, in today's inflationary environment, the pipeline alone could cost multiple billions, and this is if construction is technically possible at all. The pipeline would have to go from sea level up to a 4,000-meter elevation, to then fuel a 220-megawatt power plant to crush 53,500 tonnes per day of refractory ore—a massive industrial complex in the middle of nowhere. On top, all construction materials would either have to be barged up the Kuskokwim River or flown in. Think about the scale of it all.

At the end of the day, Donlin is a relatively old deposit that was initially supposed to enter production in 2008. Almost 20 years later, with hundreds of millions spent on general and administrative expenses, NovaGold still has no road map to reach production. Yes, there are a lot of ounces in the ground, at a pretty attractive grade, but what is it all worth if you cannot extract it?

It is also worth wondering why a company focused on advancing a single project has to spend $25 million a year on general and administrative expenses. What about the multi-million dollar compensation packages? What about 'Fee Warrants' issued to the largest shareholders in exchange for a financial backstop?

After all, if NovaGold was truly a potent call option on gold prices, how could we explain the company's relative performance versus gold over the last five years? To me, there is one very good explanation: Dilution, dilution, and more dilution. There is an endless supply of paper, and we are not even talking about construction yet.

The main reason behind my 'Hold' rating is my optimism about gold prices. Even though I would put a 'Sell' rating on the Donlin Project itself, it could still catch a bid during a precious metals bull market. To me, the NovaGold story is a reminder that finding a lot of ounces at good grades is nowhere near enough to justify building a mine. It shows how high the bar is for any grassroots exploration endeavours. More often than not, the share prices of junior miners are more dependent upon promotional activities than on any real substance.

Buyer beware!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Diamond Hill Long-Short Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets were mixed in Q1 as the new presidential administration made repeated waves with an avalanche of activity, which left markets uncertain of the likely path forward. For the quarter, US stocks fell -4.7% (as measured by the Russell 3000 Index), notching markets’ first quarterly decline since Q3 2023. Mid-cap stocks held up best, falling a more modest -3.4%, while large caps were down -4.5%.

Smaller stocks were hit harder, falling -9.5% — a relatively unsurprising development given smaller companies’ shares are generally more volatile as macroeconomic uncertainty increases. From a style perspective, value fared better up and down the cap spectrum, with large-cap value managing a positive gain of +2.1%. Mid-cap value stocks fell -2.1%, compared to -7.1% for their growth counterparts, while small-cap value stocks declined -7.7% versus -11.1% for small growth (all returns as measured by their respective Russell indices). The shift from growth to value was also relatively unsurprising given the flight to safety prompted by tremendous uncertainty around trade and other fiscal policy shifts in the US.

From a sector perspective, likewise, more defensive sectors in the Russell 1000 Index led in the quarter, while cyclical stocks fell out of favor. Energy stocks were the best performers, up +9.6% amid supportive language from President Trump’s administration about its willingness to increase US oil production. Health care also rose nicely, up +5.6%, as did utilities, which gained +5.2%. Conversely, consumer discretionary stocks were hit hardest, declining -13.2%, while information technology stocks fell -12.8% — which, given the sector’s weight in the index, was a major contributor to the index’s negative Q1 return.

What began in Q4 — market leadership transitions in anticipation of potential winners and losers from a major shift in executive-level policy direction — vigorously continued in Q1. To some extent, economic data took a backseat in the quarter as investors seemed to focus narrowly on the likeliest implications of major trade policy shifts announced during the quarter by President Trump. Shortly after the quarter concluded, Trump announced a raft of tariffs to go into effect immediately on what he dubbed “Liberation Day.” As has been the case since Trump began increasing tariffs, countries are responding in various ways, with some reaching out to the US to negotiate better rates and others — notably, China — choosing to retaliate with their own high tariff rates. Many companies have preemptively announced significant investments in their US operations — ostensibly moves aimed at avoiding tariffs themselves. Ultimately, whether the “Liberation Day” moniker goes down in history or infamy remains to be seen. What is clear is markets are very nervous about the president’s apparent determination to wholesale (and possibly singlehandedly) rework the global trade landscape, even with countries that have historically been significant trade allies, like Taiwan. We’ll have more thoughts on potential direction from here later in this communication.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held rates in March, citing the likelihood tariffs increase inflationary pressures, while cutting its growth expectations for US GDP in 2025. And the US dollar — which one would generally expect to be bolstered relative to other global currencies as tariffs go up (higher prices for US goods increase demand for US dollars with which to pay for them) — counterintuitively fell as tariff language toughened, perhaps signaling investors’ growing concerns tariffs will contribute to a stagflationary US economic environment with high inflation and low or sluggish growth.

Globally, geopolitical uncertainty remains high, though there have been some signs of modest progress toward ending the war between Russia and Ukraine. Following a dramatic fall-out between President Trump and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the US and Ukraine seemed to slowly make their way back toward a minerals and mining deal, which would meaningfully increase the US’s economic involvement in Ukraine and potentially pave the way for a broader peace deal between Russia and Ukraine. However, to date, Russian President Vladimir Putin has stymied the US’s attempts to broker a ceasefire between the two countries. War in the Middle East continues, too, as ceasefires between Israel and Hamas and Israel and Lebanon ended and violence across the region re- escalated. The US, too, struck Houthi rebels in Yemen toward the end of the quarter in an attempt to quell attacks on Red Sea shipping lanes.

While volatility is unsettling, it also introduces interesting investing opportunities for those who have been diligent in their research all along. We have no unique insight into how deep this correction will become, whether it will enter bear market territory or how long it will last. However, we know it has helped reset valuations and offered some compelling opportunities to purchase high-quality names at more attractive prices than we’ve seen in some time. Once the trade-related dust settles, time will tell which sectors and individual companies are winners and losers. In the meantime, we will continue deploying our time-tested approach, which is well-built for uncertain market environments like we’re currently seeing — and which we wouldn’t be surprised to see persist for the foreseeable future.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio outperformed the Russell 1000 Index and the blended benchmark (60% Russell 1000 Index/40% Bloomberg US Treasury Bills 1-3 Month Index). Both our short and long books provided relative tailwinds in Q1, with our short book underperforming the index and our long book positive on an absolute basis, while the index was in the red. Our long information technology exposure — both our holdings and our underweight exposure to the sector — was additive to relative performance. Our long financials and industrials holdings were also sources of relative strength.

Conversely, our long consumer staples holdings detracted from relative performance in Q1.

On an individual holdings’ basis, top contributors to return in Q1 included several positions from our long book, including American International Group (AIG), WNS Holdings (WNS) and VeriSign (VRSN). Property and casualty insurance company AIG is delivering high-quality results: Its most recently reported underlying loss ratio was better than expected, as were catastrophe losses and prior-year development. Further, the company continues generating revenue growth from both its commercial and consumer segments. We are pleased with the progress AIG is making to streamline its business and capitalize on opportunities for earnings growth better than its competitors. We believe it remains well-positioned to scale revenues efficiently and deliver sustainable value as it continues reducing expenses and returning excess capital via share repurchases.

Business process management company WNS indicated it expects to return to steady, predictable growth following several quarters of uncharacteristically volatile fundamentals, giving a boost to shares in the quarter.

VeriSign, which provides internet infrastructure and domain name registry services, successfully renewed its .com agreement with the US Department of Commerce in late 2024. It also demonstrated stability in both its .com and .net domain bases. We believe the company is favorably positioned with an attractive business model and management team, which has effectively and opportunistically allocated capital.

Also among our top individual Q1 contributors were our short positions in GameStop (GME) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK). Shares of electronics retailer GameStop were pressured in the quarter as speculative trading in the stock declined. Deckers Outdoor designs, markets and distributes casual and high- performance footwear, apparel and accessories. As competition is increasing via promotions in the running footwear space, investors are seemingly weighing whether revenue growth could begin decelerating consistently — which has weighed on shares.

Our bottom individual Q1 contributors were all from our long book, including lululemon athletica (LULU) and Ulta Beauty (ULTA). Shares of athletic apparel manufacturer lululemon were pressured amid ongoing concerns about potential brand saturation in the Americas, its largest market. However, we believe the company is operating well and is positioned to improve US sales performance while continuing to grow international sales — factors that make the current valuation compelling, in our view.

Specialty beauty retailer Ulta Beauty is delivering solid results, with earnings ahead of expectations and guidance for this year consistent. Shares seem to have declined in Q1 largely in sympathy with weaker sentiment surrounding the broad consumer discretionary sector. However, we maintain our conviction in Ulta’s long-term opportunity to take share given its compelling portfolio of beauty brands across a range of price points, including its own private-label brand.

Other bottom individual contributors in Q1 included our long positions in Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). Software and information technology services provider Microsoft faced some investor caution around the possibility that artificial intelligence- and cloud computing-related spending could decelerate faster than previously anticipated. Media and technology company Alphabet faces GenAI competition to its search business from rivals such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Perplexity and others. However, we believe the advent of GenAI should expand the addressable search market, and Alphabet is responding proactively to increased competition with equal or superior products while managing ongoing antitrust challenges, diversifying its revenue base with other Google products and reducing costs.

Shares of semiconductor manufacturer Taiwan Semiconductor underperformed amid concerns about slowing near-term artificial intelligence demand following two years of rapid growth. Geopolitical and tariff-related headwinds also likely pressured shares — however, we believe these concerns are temporary and maintain our constructive long-term outlook on the company.

Portfolio Activity

As volatility picked up sharply in the quarter, we were active in the portfolio — and we anticipate that as volatility continues into Q2, we will likewise attempt to capitalize on compelling opportunities to reposition the portfolio for the period ahead. Among the new positions we initiated in Q1 were long positions in Salesforce and Capital One, as well as short positions in Matson and eBay.

Salesforce (CRM) provides customer relationship management (CRM) technology to companies globally. We believe the company is well-positioned to reverse recently unfavorable cyclicality and capitalize on go-to-market enhancements, price increases and GenAI revenue. As the company evolves its go-to-market strategy, shifts its sales channel mix, consolidates its tech stack and transitions to third-party data centers, we anticipate Salesforce should meaningfully expand margins. Shares were trading at an attractive discount relative to our estimate of intrinsic value, and so we capitalized on the opportunity to initiate a position.

Capital One (COF) is one of the US’s largest credit card issuers and is known for having the industry’s best technology platform, which has helped drive improved operating efficiency and strong underwriting, and helps in refreshing card offerings — a key to maintaining its position in a highly competitive industry. Capital One announced the acquisition of Discover in early 2024 — a deal which should unlock growth potential as it gets Capital One closer to merchants and gives the company access to the data necessary to create more competitive card offerings than peers. Though the merger still awaits regulatory approval, we believe the current share price fails to appreciate the near- and long- term synergies once the deal is approved, and accordingly, we capitalized on the attractive valuation to initiate a position in the quarter.

Matson (MATX) is an ocean container shipping company servicing US Jones Act routes (routes regulated by federal law mandating goods shipped between US ports be transported on ships built, owned and operated by US citizens or permanent residents) in Hawaii and Alaska, as well as smaller routes in Guam, Japan, New Zealand and

Micronesia. It also provides express shipping services from China to Long Beach, CA, which is its most profitable route. The company has recently benefited from several temporary tailwinds, including Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping, port strikes and the threat of tariffs, which have pulled forward some shipping. However, we anticipate these tailwinds will reverse and industry capacity will increase while demand decreases — factors which should pressure Matson’s earnings and, with it, the share price. Accordingly, we initiated a short position in the quarter.

eBay (EBAY) operates marketplace platforms connecting buyers and sellers in a handful of major global markets, including the US, UK, China and Germany. Though the company’s fundamentals have improved over the last several quarters, eBay is still losing share to numerous competitors. We consequently anticipate the company will need to invest to maintain its fundamentals and growth. With shares overvalued, in our view, we initiated a short position in Q1.

We exited our long positions in property and casualty insurance company Allstate (ALL) and frozen potato products company Lamb Weston in favor of more compelling investing opportunities elsewhere. We also sold our long position in banking and financial services company Wells Fargo as the share price approached our estimate of intrinsic value.

We also covered several shorts in Q1, including in electrical infrastructure products manufacturer Atkore, cosmetic and skin care products provider e.l.f. Beauty (ELF) and information technology services provider Akamai Technologies (AKAM) as their share prices fell to our estimates of intrinsic value. We covered our short positions in maintenance and repair products provider W.W. Grainger (GWW) and enterprise information technology company Oracle in favor of more compelling opportunities elsewhere.

The portfolio’s net exposure at the end of the quarter was 57.8%.

Market Outlook

As we start Q2, uncertainty is as high as it has been, arguably since the pandemic’s earliest days. While markets struggle to digest the likeliest ramifications of the US’s newly announced tariffs — whether they are largely a negotiation tactic whose economic impact will moderate over time as countries and companies reach better terms with the US, or whether they are here to stay for the long term and we will indeed see a significant reshaping of the world trade order — we are watching several factors particularly closely. Foremost, we are monitoring the relationship between the US and China, which is front and center in this trade showdown. As we write, President Trump has threatened a 104% tariff on China in response to its retaliatory increase to 84%. Given the magnitude of the US and Chinese economies, seeing the two countries enter a full-blown trade war would have significant, widespread implications for many US companies, up and down the capitalization spectrum.

Further, the increased uncertainty across a range of macroeconomic policies — trade as well as domestic spending and the administration’s ongoing efforts to meaningfully shrink the size of the federal government — is prompting concerns about the consumer. Should tariffs prompt a significant spike in inflation and/or fiscal spending cuts go too far, consumers could rein in their spending.

Indeed, companies across various sectors have noted a weakening consumer, suggesting the economy is slowing. With the consumer powering a significant majority of the economy, further weakening may present a risk to the lofty double-digit corporate earnings growth the market had been expecting for 2025.

However, regardless of how uncomfortable such volatility can be, we are simultaneously encouraged by its impact on the valuations of some companies that we believe are particularly strong and well-positioned to weather the environment ahead — even if it requires continued challenges before economic and market growth resume.

Accordingly, we have sought to be active in this period, identifying high-quality companies with particularly solid balance sheets whose outlook over the next three to five years is positive in our view. As downward volatility has continued, we have further acted on our research efforts to add these companies to the portfolio, with the aim of positioning the portfolio well for whatever the environment gives us in the period ahead — whether there is further downside to endure, or whether markets turn around precipitously. We believe, with hindsight, that this period may prove to be one that separates those investors with a rigorous, bottom-up process from those unwilling or unable to see through the near-term volatility to the possibilities beyond.

Our primary focus is always on achieving value-added results for our clients, and we believe we can achieve better- than-market returns over the next five years through active portfolio management.

data.title: Oracle: AI Juggernaut Starts Delivering On Its Promises
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Oracle Corporation (NYSE: NYSE:ORCL), back in March 2025, I analyzed the company’s third quarter earnings report and investigated whether there had been any significant developments regarding the Stargate initiative. I also examined the existing demand for OCI to see if there are any cracks. I had a Buy rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has surged, gaining 35%, comfortably outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained nearly 8% during the same period.

In this article, I dissect the company’s Q4 report and explore the key takeaways, especially with respect to the demand for OCI as well as how the company is dealing with the tariff-riddled macroenvironment that it currently operates in.

A Snapshot of Oracle’s Q4 Results

ORCL bucked its recent trend by beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $15.9 billion, up 11.3% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $310 million. Non-GAAP EPS of $1.70 beat analyst estimates by $0.06. The current quarter also saw revenue growth nearly doubling compared to the previous quarter. Cloud infrastructure delivered yet another quarter of remarkable growth, jumping 52% y/y. Non-GAAP operating margins came in at 44%, a decline of 300 bps y/y but flat on a sequential basis.

Over the last twelve months, the company generated $20.82 billion in operating cash flows. As a result of a surge in capital expenditures, which came in at $21.2 billion in Q4, free cash flows, however, turned negative. Finally, management maintained their quarterly dividend at $0.50 per share. For the full year, ORCL generated revenues of $57.4 billion, up 8% y/y, and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $6.03, which translates to a y/y growth of 8.4%.

With respect to first quarter guidance, management now sees total revenues growing between 11% and 13% y/y in constant currency, with total cloud revenues expected to register a y/y growth in the range between 26% and 30%. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to grow between 5% to 7% and come in the range of $1.46 and $1.50, assuming a tax rate of 19%. Management also projected FY26 revenue growth to double and come in at $67 billion, on the back of a projected RPO growth of more than 100% y/y.

Q4 Shows Company is Starting to Deliver on Its Promises & There’s More to Come

There has been a lot of debate on the amount of spending that the AI players have undertaken, especially with many of them yet to fully translate the spending into meaningful growth. When it comes to ORCL however, there should be no such debate in my opinion. This, to me, was the main takeaway from the company’s Q4 report. In other words, management not only promised investors that their spending would translate into growth but also delivered on the promise.

One of the metrics that offer evidence on this is the Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO). This metric has seen a remarkable growth in recent quarters. Q4 was no different as the metric stood at $138 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 41%. That’s an increase of $8 billion from the previous quarter and is the second straight quarter when this metric has crossed $100 billion. 33% of the RPOs are projected to be recognized as revenue in the next 12 months, which translates to $45.5 billion.

Despite all these blowout numbers, investors can still point to the fact that the RPO growth has dramatically declined from 63% y/y in Q3 to 41% y/y in Q4. This deceleration is the evidence that the management has delivered on its promise of translating their investments into meaningful growth.

As seen from the above figure, the decline in RPO growth can be attributed to more capacity coming online, which enabled the company to convert RPOs into revenues. The company’s investments have paid off to such an extent that the it's revenues hit double-digits for the first time in five quarters.

Moreover, despite the company’s investments translating into actual growth, based on management commentary, demand continues to outstrip supply. As such, the company is not slowing down its spending. After spending $21.2 billion in capital expenditures in FY24, the company now projects FY25 Capex to be in excess of $25 billion. Management, in my opinion, has every right to boost its spending. This is because not only is revenue growth expected to nearly double from 8.4% in FY25 to 16% in FY26 but RPO, which is the ideal indicator of demand, is expected to grow more than 100% in FY26. In absolute terms, this would translate to more than $275 billion. Given that RPOs are noncancelable contracts, this offers further evidence as to why ORCL needs to keep spending. At the same time, based on the revenue growth trajectory, investors can take solace in the fact that the company’s investments are actually translating into growth. Not many AI players can offer evidence as concrete as ORCL.

Moreover, management commentary also suggested that Stargate’s formation is still not complete, and as such, the massive FY26 RPO number doesn’t involve any meaningful contribution from Stargate. In my last article on ORCL, I pointed out that any growth via Stargate is a bonus for ORCL. The fact that this remains the case even in Q4 is promising in my opinion.

Lastly, investors should also have no concerns about any delays in ORCL's capacity coming online. During the earnings call, management categorically stated that they have no issues in obtaining the GPUs, which should offer investors more confidence in the management’s revenue projections for both FY26 and even FY27.

Taking all these together, Q4 proved that ORCL continues to be an AI juggernaut. And with the company’s other segments also firing on all cylinders, except for Services, investors, in my opinion would be hard-pressed to find any meaningful weakness in its AI journey.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

$206.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

32.1x

PV of Projected FY26 EPS

$6.45

Source: Company’s Q4FY25 Earnings Call Transcript, LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), and Author’s Calculations.

ORCL, according to LSEG Data, currently trades at forward P/E of 25.9x, which makes it more expensive compared to Salesforce (CRM) and IBM (IBM) but cheaper than Workday (WDAY) and SAP (SAP). Relative to its historical levels, the company is more expensive, given that it’s 5-year median forward P/E multiple stands at 17.6x. However, since ORCL has undergone a transformation in the AI era, the company deserves a multiple re-rating to the upside in my opinion.

For FY25, the company registered an EPS growth of 8.4%, as mentioned earlier. This falls short of its long-term mean EPS growth of 10.64% but is above its trailing 5-year CAGR of 7.13%. Furthermore, if one compares the mean long-term EPS growth of ORCL and its peers, then ORCL, in my opinion, should be trading at a similar multiple to CRM.

In my last article on CRM, I had assumed a forward P/E of 32.1x for the company. As such, I have assumed the same multiple (32.1x) for ORCL, slightly higher than my previous estimate of 31.9x.

According to Seeking Alpha, ORCL currently trades at a forward PEG ratio of 2.95, well above both the sector median (1.79) and its 5-year average (1.91). The table below shows the projected FY26 EPS based on these different PEG ratios. The forward P/E multiple remains at 32.1x and the FY25 EPS remains at $6.03 for all three scenarios.

Using a discount factor of 8.44%, obtained form Alpha Spread, the present value of the projected FY26 EPS comes in at $6.41. At a forward P/E of 32.1x, the price target would then be $206. At the time of writing this article, shares have already crossed $200, which implies that there is limited upside. This cannot be surprising given that the stock would be up more than 20% YTD if it finishes around the $200 levels today. According to Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale, the stock has decent momentum in the short-term, as reflected by a grade of B+. Taking things together, I am downgrading my rating on the stock from a Hold to a Buy.

Despite the downgrade, I continue to love the company. The performance of this company has been remarkable, and I don’t see any reason why the company would slow down. My rating downgrade is purely a valuation call. Should the stock see any pullback, then in my opinion, ORCL is one name that investors should prioritize adding to their portfolios.

Concluding Thoughts

ORCL finished the quarter strongly, beating on both the top- and bottom-lines, thereby reversing its recent trend of misses. The demand growth for OCI continues to remain strong and one of the key takeaways of this quarter is that this demand growth is getting translated into revenue growth.

The company’s investments are starting to pay off, as evidenced by the first quarter of double-digit revenue growth for the company and the projected FY26 revenue growth expected to double y/y. At the same time, the surging RPO number shows that there is no fatigue in demand and given that the company is not facing any issues in getting GPUs to build the data centers, the company should be able to cater to this demand without any major issues.

My only concern with respect to ORCL is the valuation. Post earnings release, the stocks has already surged nearly 13% and crossed the $200 mark. As such, there is limited upside at current levels, based on my assumptions.

Having said that, while I am downgrading my rating on the stock from a Buy to a Hold, this is purely a valuation call and is targeted towards investors who are yet to get in the name. I don’t plan to sell any of my shares yet as momentum in the stock has certainly improved. Given that the momentum in demand is also translating into revenue momentum, this improvement shouldn’t come as a surprise to investors.
